speaker: interviewee
text: So we managed to get through those fence, and we came close to the border, to the Russian-Polish border. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] By the way, I always remember that the place, it was Malkin, but it-- in my, in my mind, it was a story, you know. I knew there was a place Malkin, and we passed it, and there was the border, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but two years ago when I was in Poland and on the way to Treblinka, they stopped the bus, and they said, here is Malkin, Malkinia in Polish. And I said, oh, that's where it was, now. Now this place became more real in my mind. OK, so we, we came to the, to the Russian border, and we were sitting there with thousands and thousands of people. And we couldn't get through the other side. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Finally, somebody told my mother-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] it was a kind of an isolated place and there weren't much houses around it, but in the distance we could see some isolated places, houses where people were, were living.

speaker: interviewer
text: And while we were sitting there for about two weeks, as I said, somebody told my mother, you know, why don't you try to get the soldiers to let you through? You just ask him that you are here with two little children, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you didn't wash them, and they didn't have anything warm for, for them. Maybe they let you get into the house. Tell them after that you'll come back. And if you succeed, once you are there, you'll see, maybe you can continue to go. So my mother tried it, and-- first, first of all, you couldn't get close to any soldier, you know. There was Russian soldiers there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You couldn't get close to them. Once you got close, they started to, to yell and to, to threaten with their pistols and [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: But after a few tries, I guess, she succeeded in getting close to a soldier. And she begged him and asked him. And he said, OK, you come back later. Yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we managed to get past the other, to the other side and go into a hut. Was more than a-- more a hut than a house, you know. And get into that place, and we asked the lady-- was a lady there, a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Russian, or Ukrainian, I guess Ukrainian, or Polish. I'm not really sure-- for to stay over the night and to wash ourselves a little. And she agreed, and she, she gave us some food.

speaker: interviewee
text: And while we were there, she explained that-- she said, you know, every day around 12 o'clock or so-- I don't know exactly the time-- but around noon time or 12 o'clock, the guards change. While they change they are not really watching much, you know. So that would be-- between this hour-- it would be a good time. You just go and don't pay attention. Continue to walk. And if you are lucky they won't stop you. You will just be on the other side. And it wasn't a big, a big distance. It was a distance, but not that big. And that's what we did, and we succeeded. This is how we came over to the other side. And we came-- that was the Ukraine, but by that time it was already Russia because that was the agreement between Russia and Germany at that time, that they get one part of Poland, and the Germans get the other part of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got, we got to the other side, and we managed to get to that city, which was Rivne, which my father was supposed to be. And we had an address there where he was supposed to be there, too. It was a friend of my father, a colleague.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they told us the story. Because that friend was working in [NON-ENGLISH] and, and another friend, the three of them, they escaped. My father was shot. They managed to, to escape and to run. So we came to this guy's place, and he told us that our father was killed on the way. So that's when we learned that he was killed. So now we were again by ourselves. There were a lot of refugees there. Living [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] quarters were very hard to get. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And to work, even harder. And there was no money. It was very difficult. So my mother was looking for something to do, and then she hear that the, the Russians are taking people [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] farther into Russia, and they giving them work. So she registered, and we went there.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We were there, I don't know for how long. But it must have been only a short time, some months. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was actually not bad. We got an apartment there. My mother got to work in a restaurant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in Rivne?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was in Russia, outside-- in Russia already. That was after Rivne. I don't even know the name of the place, where it was. I remember the place. I remember the apartment where we were living. I remember the grounds, the surroundings. But I really don't remember even the name of the place. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I went there-- still I continue to go there to kindergarten. And my mother got a job in the local restaurant. There were no private restaurant. This was Russia under communist regime, so it was a local restaurant. She got a job there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had where to sleep, and we had food, and, and I was going to school, which I was enjoying as a child, you know, so-- Did you have a problem with the language?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. I picked up the language right away. Right away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as a matter of fact, when we came-- after, after we left this place, for some reason, I don't know why, my mother decided to, to go back to Rivne. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We left that place. Maybe she, she wanted to go back to Poland, or so. Because we hear that they are registering people if they want to go back to Poland. Maybe she wanted to go back since my father wasn't alive, and there we had, she had her brother and sister and mother, and I had my grandmother from my father's side, too. So maybe that was the reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she registers to go back, and we came back to Rivne. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember it was a Friday when we came back. How long were you in that other place for, where you went to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said it, it wasn't long. It could have been maybe some--

speaker: interviewer
text: A few months?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few months, yes. And-- so we came back from that place. We came back to Rivne, and it was a Friday. And as soon as we came back, the Russians took us. We were registered to go back to Poland, so they took us, and they put us on the train. It was no passenger train. It was a cattle train. It was a huge transport of tens of cattle [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] cars. And, and they put us there. And they put us on the way to, to Siberia. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And about the language that you ask before, I, I was already by that time so fluent in Russian-- as much as a six-year-old can be, you know, fluent in a language-- but fluent enough to keep a very welcome conversation. And since they put us-- in, in, in every car they put a Russian soldier.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were maybe 40, 50 people in a, in a car. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And nobody knew, knew Russian. So I was the translator between the Russian soldier and the whole, and the whole car of, the whole car of people. So that's how fast I obtained the language, and it was no problem.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took us to Siberia, a trip which took some months. And it was a huge transport of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tens and tens of-- maybe even hundreds of, of cars. Huge. Thousands of people. Many thousands of people that they took.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't want-- the Russians, from what I hear, they, they didn't want so many foreign people on their borders, because they were afraid of espionage. So that was their official explanation later, from what I understand. So they took them all, and they took them to Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stop at places along the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to stop every so often and get some [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] hot soup and some provisions and water [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] supply in, in every car. And we might have stopped-- I don't know-- some places hours, some places maybe days, or some places maybe only overnight. You know, those are details that as a child I can't tell. I guess to me at that time, time wasn't something to measure, you know. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And they-- we-- it took us a, a long time until we reached our destination and, and they took us off the train. I guess we were taken to a place. I guess, where we got off was Novosibirsk, a place that's a, a town. Novosibirsk, that's the capital of that region. And from there, we were taken by all kind of transportation to some villages deeper in Siberia. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] In Siberia we had very bad conditions, very bad. Food was in very short supply.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you with at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my mother and my brother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Food was in very short supply. We had no money. Many people were with money so they could help themselves this way. They could buy some product, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] food, bread, some-- they were some Russian people that they were living there in that, in the village, and they had their own fields so they had potatoes and all kinds of stuff. So they had meat, pigs that they would sell. But that was for money. Since we didn't have money, we couldn't buy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning when we arrived there, as I said, there was a village there, and they, they gave every-- they made every villager there accept one family, or two. I don't know. But we stayed in, in one with a lady that, she was by herself, I think. She had-- yeah. She had a son and a daughter, but they weren't living with her. They were somewhere else. And she was by herself. So we, we were in, in that house with that lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. She was a nice lady, you know. She, she shared with us whatever she, she had. And we stayed with her and-- until they built there a, a special village for all those people that they brought. And the people that they brought, they, they had to work there to build those houses. I remember they were-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] by the time it was finished, it was like a whole new village built. Rows and rows of houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I attended school there, actually. I finished my first grade in Siberia. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I was good in reading and writing Russian. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And, and I actually started my second grade in Siberia, too. By the time we were only in the beginning of the second grade, we were allowed to leave Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like for you at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. In school, I was a, I was a good student. I was very, [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] I was very punctual to come on time to school, since by some bad luck or coincidence-- I don't know-- we got the last, the last house in the, the last house in the last row, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in, in that village. It was right beside the forest. And it was a distance away from, from the school. And we didn't have-- I didn't have much clothes, and it was a way-- quite a distance to go, so I used to start very early so I will not be late to, to class. So I always used to come the first to school. The teacher of the school, she-- of my class, actually-- she was also the principal of the school. And since I was the first child to always arrive, she probably liked me, too, I don't know. So, she used to-- she lived in that building. So she used to take me in, into her apartment. She was living there with a husband, and she had a, a small child, a small child, a daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the teacher's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I must have known but by now, no. I remember only that she was a short, very nice woman. I liked her very much. And-- so she used to take me into her apartment. And in one of those mornings, I guess after we became very close-- and I knew that we are in Russia and we are-- and, and Russia is a communist country. And so as a child, I wanted to brag about, and so I told her, you know, my father was a communist, which he was. And he was involved in, in the Communist Party in Poland, you know. So-- and he was jailed with Gomulka and all the, those leaders. In later years they told me that if he would have stayed alive, he would have been probably one of the leaders of Poland after the war in the Communist Party.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, so, I told my teacher about it, and I was very surprised by her reaction. And she said, OK, you told me this. But this is between four walls. Nobody heard it. And you're a smart boy. If you want to avoid any problems to you and your mother, don't talk about it anymore. I was seven years old when she told me that. I remember it. Did she know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes. She knew. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we stayed in, in Siberia, very bad conditions. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] People if they, if they ran out of money, they had jewelry. If they ran out of jewelry, they had clothes, old clothes or clothes that they could spare. And all this was a lot of, worth a lot of money in the-- not money, but food, which was more than money in, in, in Russia and Siberia, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in Siberia a year and a half. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was one episode that I remember about going to school in Siberia. I was in grade one and, as I said, I was always-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] always made sure that I come early to school. So I was early. I left early in the morning, and we were quite a distance away from the school. I didn't have much clothes. I used to put some rags around my shoes, whatever shoes they were, and this way I used to come to school. Because it was very cold winter. The snow was high. The winds were terrible. And I, on my way to school, I passed-- there was a, a, a well, a well, a water supply for, for that area, you know. And when I came to that well there was-- lucky for me, there was a, a lady at the well that she knew me. And I stopped to say good morning, you know. And as soon as she saw me, she grabbed me into her house, which was just across the [INAUDIBLE]. And she started to rub me. And it turned out that my nose was half frozen already by then. And she started to-- she noticed that it's getting all white and, and this, this way she, she saved my nose. If I would have probably continued all the way to school, I probably would have lost my nose by then because it was still a distance to go. That was an episode of going to school in Siberia. But we stayed Siberia a year and a half, and it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember if you encountered anti-Semitism there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not, not in Russia then. [DRINKS] Not in Russia then for a simple reason. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Stalin was very, very [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] tough about any kind of a, of a, any kind of a personal respond, if it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't have a problem there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you leave Siberia?

speaker: interviewee
text: We left Siberia-- it was '42. It was '42. We left Siberia. And from Siberia, we went to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember why you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were, we were allowed to, to leave Siberia after the Germans attacked Russia. And there was the treaty signed between Britain, the United States, and, and France. And since the, there was a Polish government in London at that time-- there was the Polish government in exile, it was called. And they were part of the treaty, too. So they were-- so the Polish citizens weren't anymore, like, kind of enemies of Russia, so they were allowed to leave Siberia. And we were, we were given the right to choose where we want to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, since we had enough of the, the Siberia's winters, so I guess most of the people chose to go to warm countries. And that's where we went to. We chose to go to Uzbekistan, which, which was in Asia. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and if we had it bad in Siberia, we had it triple bad later on after we left Siberia. Again, the way from Siberia, the way to-- the trip from Siberia to, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to Uzbekistan was, again, a very hard one. Because you have to buy tickets. You don't have money, you can't buy tickets. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We started, we started to leave Siberia by foot. We were walking about-- to the first [INAUDIBLE], we had about 1,000 kilometers to walk. And that's how we started to walk, 1,000 kilometers through forest. And we would walk forests for days. Once in a while we would come across a horse and buggy would give us a lift for, for an hour or two. We would continue. Get off. Walk again. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Get another lift. And that's how it went on for, for weeks, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, what happened when you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had, we had to come back to again to Novosibirsk. That's where we could pick up transportation, train transportation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we, we reach Novosibirsk and that's, OK, now we can get a train. But you need the money, so we didn't have money. So we started to, to catch, to, to get ourselves on, on [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] passenger trains-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not on passenger, on transport trains, cattle trains, transport trains, all kind of trains, which was a very dangerous thing to do in Russia, especially at the wartime. If you were caught you could have got shot right on the spot.

speaker: interviewee
text: And to our luck we were, we were caught on the way, once by an [INAUDIBLE], and he, right away he wanted to part us with our mother, take our [INAUDIBLE] to the jail. And I was sent away in some orphanage.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so we were-- my mother started to cry. We started to cry. And then my mother said, you, you're going to take me and put me to jail? My husband is on the front and fighting, and you're going to-- oh, your husband's on the front? So that saved us. And he put us on passenger train all the way to, to our destination-- almost all the way to our destination. So this is how, actually, we arrived, so-- So we arrived there and then, again, started-- we-- our place was Samarkand in Uzbekistan. And we, we came there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And again, you have nowhere to stay and no food and that. So we, we choose to go to a kolkhoz. Kolkhoz, that is a co-op, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] agricultural co-op. OK, let's stop here.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
